text: The U.S. federal income tax system uses progressive marginal rates. For 2024, the top marginal rate is 37% for income over $578,125 (single) or $693,750 (married filing jointly). Additional considerations include the 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) on investment income for high earners, which applies when modified adjusted gross income exceeds $200,000 (single) or $250,000 (married filing jointly).

Long-term capital gains (assets held >1 year) are taxed at preferential rates: 0%, 15%, or 20%, depending on taxable income. The 20% rate applies when taxable income exceeds $492,300 (single) or $553,850 (married filing jointly) in 2024. High earners may also owe the 3.8% NIIT on capital gains. Short-term capital gains (assets held ≤1 year) are taxed as ordinary income at marginal rates up to 37%.

Tax-loss harvesting involves selling securities at a loss to offset capital gains and reduce taxable income. Capital losses can offset capital gains dollar-for-dollar. If losses exceed gains, up to $3,000 of excess loss can be deducted against ordinary income annually, with remaining losses carried forward indefinitely. Wash sale rules prohibit claiming a loss if a substantially identical security is purchased within 30 days before or after the sale.

Qualified Opportunity Zones (QOZs) offer tax incentives for investing capital gains into designated economically distressed communities. Benefits include: (1) temporary deferral of capital gains tax until 2026 or until the investment is sold, (2) step-up in basis of 10% if held 5+ years (15% if 7+ years) before 2026, and (3) permanent exclusion of capital gains on the QOZ investment if held for at least 10 years. Investments must be made through Qualified Opportunity Funds (QOFs).

Section 1031 like-kind exchanges allow deferral of capital gains tax when selling investment or business real property and reinvesting proceeds into similar property. The replacement property must be identified within 45 days and the exchange completed within 180 days. Key benefits include: (1) indefinite deferral of capital gains, (2) increased purchasing power by using tax savings, (3) portfolio diversification, and (4) estate planning advantages through stepped-up basis at death. Only real property qualifies after the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act.

Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs) provide immediate tax deductions while allowing flexible timing of charitable distributions. Contributions of appreciated securities to a DAF avoid capital gains tax and provide a charitable deduction for the full fair market value (up to 30% of AGI for appreciated assets, 60% for cash). DAFs are particularly valuable in high-income years to maximize tax benefits, and allow strategic bunching of multiple years of charitable giving into one tax year.

Choice of business entity significantly impacts taxation. S-Corporations allow pass-through taxation while potentially reducing self-employment taxes on distributions. C-Corporations face double taxation but benefit from the flat 21% corporate rate and qualified dividends treatment. Limited Liability Companies (LLCs) offer flexibility to be taxed as sole proprietorships, partnerships, or corporations. For high-income business owners, S-Corp election can save 15.3% self-employment tax on profit distributions exceeding reasonable compensation.

Tax-advantaged retirement accounts include Traditional 401(k)/IRA (pre-tax contributions, taxable withdrawals) and Roth 401(k)/IRA (after-tax contributions, tax-free withdrawals). For 2024, 401(k) contribution limits are $23,000 ($30,500 if age 50+). High earners may use backdoor Roth conversions to circumvent income limits. Mega backdoor Roth allows up to $69,000 in total 401(k) contributions through after-tax contributions and in-plan conversions. Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) begin at age 73.

State income tax rates vary significantly and should be considered in comprehensive tax planning. States with no income tax include Florida, Texas, Nevada, Washington, Wyoming, South Dakota, Alaska, Tennessee, and New Hampshire. High-tax states like California (top rate 13.3%), New York (10.9%), and New Jersey (10.75%) may justify relocation strategies for high-net-worth individuals. State residency rules typically require spending >183 days in a state to establish domicile.

The federal estate tax exemption is $13.61 million per individual ($27.22 million per married couple) in 2024, indexed for inflation. Estates exceeding this threshold are taxed at 40%. The current high exemption is scheduled to sunset after 2025, potentially reverting to ~$7 million (inflation-adjusted). Strategies to reduce estate tax include: annual gifting ($18,000 per recipient in 2024), irrevocable trusts, family limited partnerships, and charitable remainder trusts. Portability allows a surviving spouse to use a deceased spouse's unused exemption.

The Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) is a parallel tax system designed to ensure high-income taxpayers pay a minimum tax amount. For 2024, AMT exemptions are $85,700 (single) and $133,300 (married filing jointly), phasing out at higher incomes. Common AMT triggers include: large state and local tax deductions (capped at $10,000), incentive stock options (ISOs), private activity bonds, and certain deductions. AMT rates are 26% on income up to $232,600 and 28% above. Tax planning should model both regular and AMT scenarios.

Inherited assets receive a 'step-up' in cost basis to fair market value as of the date of death, eliminating capital gains tax on appreciation during the decedent's lifetime. This makes holding appreciated assets until death tax-efficient from an income tax perspective (though estate tax may apply). Step-up applies to most capital assets including stocks, real estate, and collectibles. Community property states provide additional benefits with full step-up on community property assets. This rule makes gifting appreciated property during life less attractive than bequeathing at death from a capital gains perspective.
